title: Observation of flat-bottom U-shaped energy gap in high-Tc nickelate (La,Pr)3Ni2O7 thin films
authors: Zhen Liang, Tianheng Wei, Wei Ren, Haoran Ji, Zheyuan Xie, Yanzhao Liu, Ziqiang Wang, Jian Wang
abstract: The discovery of high transition temperature (high-Tc) superconductivity in Ruddlesden-Popper (R-P) bilayer nickelates under high pressure has stimulated extensive work to understand the underlying mechanism and search for superconductors with higher Tc. The recent realization of superconductivity in R-P bilayer nickelate thin films with onset Tc above 40 K at ambient-pressure enables the use of a wide array of powerful experimental tools to investigate the unconventional high-Tc superconductivity in bilayer nickelates. Here, using ultra-low temperature scanning tunneling microscopy/spectroscopy (STM/S) and electrical transport study, we report the first successful observation of an energy-symmetric, flat-bottom U-shaped gap with zero residual density of states around the Fermi level in the high-Tc nickelate (La,Pr)3Ni2O7 thin film grown on SrLaAlO4 substrate. Before and after STM/S studies, transport measurements on the same sample reveal consistent superconducting behaviors showing zero resistance, with an onset Tc above 40 K and zero resistance Tc above 20 K. The tunneling spectra exhibit highly unconventional temperature evolution, characterized by a rapid filling of the U-shaped energy gap to a V-shaped gap as the temperature increases. Furthermore, the U-shaped energy gap is reduced under a c-axis magnetic field of 14 T. The energy-symmetric U-shaped gap, taken together with its dependence on magnetic field and temperature, is consistent with the behavior of a superconducting gap, suggesting a nodeless gap function at ultra-low temperatures. Our findings shed new lights on the nature of high-Tc superconductivity and provide an encouraging and thought-provoking hint for a local superconductivity with Tc above liquid nitrogen boiling temperature in nickelate superconductors at ambient or zero pressure.
summary: This work reports the first observation of a distinct, flat-bottom U-shaped energy gap in high-Tc nickelate thin films using STM/S. By analyzing the gap's temperature and magnetic field dependence, the authors argue that the material exhibits unconventional, strong-coupling superconductivity with a large gap, suggesting potential for high critical temperatures at ambient pressure.
main_problem: The study aims to understand the underlying mechanism of high-Tc superconductivity in nickelate superconductors and characterize the nature of the superconducting energy gap.
main_result: The researchers reported the first observation of an energy-symmetric, flat-bottom U-shaped gap in the nickelate film, whose temperature and magnetic field dependence is consistent with a superconducting gap function.
method: The study employed ultra-low temperature Scanning Tunneling Microscopy/Spectroscopy (STM/S) and electrical transport measurements on the thin films.
model_or_system: The physical system is (La,Pr)3Ni2O7 thin films grown on a SrLaAlO4 substrate. The analysis probes the superconducting gap structure, which is compared against theoretical expectations for strong-coupling superconductivity.
key_observables: Energy-symmetric, flat-bottom U-shaped energy gap; zero residual density of states at the Fermi level; gap reduction under a c-axis magnetic field; U-shaped to V-shaped gap evolution with temperature.
important_parameters: Onset $T_c$ above 40 K; Gap size $\Delta \sim 41.6 	ext{ meV}$; Strong-coupling ratio $2\Delta/k_B T_c \approx 20$; Magnetic field $B = 14 	ext{ T}$.
main_assumptions: The observed gap structure is assumed to be superconducting, ruling out alternative origins like Coulomb blockade, and the analysis relies on careful deconvolution of thermal broadening effects.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the U-shaped gap structure, its suppression by a perpendicular magnetic field, and the temperature evolution from U-shaped to V-shaped spectra.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first establishing the superconducting transport properties, then using STM/S to observe the characteristic U-shaped gap at low temperatures, followed by analyzing the gap's evolution with temperature and magnetic field, and finally comparing the results to theoretical models of strong-coupling superconductivity.
why_interesting: The observation of a large, unconventional superconducting gap ($\Delta$) and the strong-coupling ratio ($\gg$ BCS limit) in a nickelate system provides crucial experimental data for theories attempting to explain high-Tc superconductivity beyond the cuprate paradigm.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1 | SC nickelate (La,Pr)3Ni2O7 thin film grown on SrLaAlO4 substrate. a, The schematic of the (La,Pr)3Ni2O7 crystal structure. b, The R-T curve of the (La,Pr)3Ni2O7 thin film measured before STM/S experiments. The onset SC Tc is 46.3 K and the zero resistance Tc is 21.7 K. The inset shows the measurement configuration. c, A typical tunneling spectrum measured at the position marked by the red star in the inset (Vs = 100 mV, Is = 500 pA, Vmod = 2 mV, T = 60 mK). The gap size (Δ) is around 41.6 meV determined by one half of the separation in energy between the two coherence peaks. Fig. 2 | U-shaped energy gap structure and reduction of the energy gap under magnetic field B = 14 T perpendicular to sample surface. a, A topographic image of the (La,Pr)3Ni2O7 thin film in Region 1 (77.71 × 77.71 nm2, Vs = 8 V, Is = 100 pA). b, The tunneling conductance spectra measured along the red arrow shown in a without magnetic field (Vs = 100 mV, Is = 500 pA, Vmod = 2 mV, T = 50 mK). The spectra are vertically shifted for clarity. c, The tunneling spectra measured along the same arrow in a under a magnetic field B = 14 T perpendicular to the surface (Vs = 100 mV, Is = 500 pA, Vmod = 2mV, T = 50 mK). The spectra are vertically shifted for clarity. d, The spatially average of the...

Fig. 3 | Tunneling spectra measured at different temperatures. a, The temperature dependence of the tunneling spectra from 60 mK to 45 K measured at the position marked by the red star in Fig.1c (Vs = 100 mV, Is = 500 pA, Vmod = 2 mV). b-c, The same spectra in a vertically shifted for clarity. The spectra measured from 60 mK to 7 K, from 10 K to 45 K are shown in b, c, respectively. The gray spectra are obtained by convoluting the spectrum measured at 60 mK by the thermal broadening functions at the corresponding temperatures.
